name: Dropbox
overview:
Dropbox is a cloud file storage and synchronization service that allows users to upload, download, share, and
sync files across multiple devices. The system must handle large file uploads efficiently (chunking, resumable
uploads), sync file changes across devices in near-real-time, support file sharing with access control, and scale
to billions of files. The read/write ratio is roughly 1:1 for sync workloads, with large files (videos, backups)
dominating storage but small files (documents) dominating operation count.

inScope: Upload files up to 5GB with resumable upload support
Sync file changes across all user devices within 30 seconds
Share files/folders with other users (read/write permissions)

items: File sync across devices within 30 seconds of a change
Upload/download throughput optimized for large files; chunk deduplication reduces bandwidth by ~90% for incremental changes
99.9% availability; data durability is paramount — no file loss
Eventual consistency for sync notifications; strong consistency for file metadata (version, ownership)
CDN cache hit rate >99% for downloads; presigned S3 URLs bypass API servers for large transfers

entities.name: File
entities.schema:
{
  file_id: UUID,
  owner_id: string,

  name: string,
  size: bytes,
  mime_type: string,
  s3_key: string,
  checksum: SHA256,
  version: int,
  created_at: timestamp,
  updated_at: timestamp
}

entities.name: Chunk
entities.schema:
{
  chunk_id: SHA256 of chunk content,
  file_id: UUID,
  chunk_index: int,
  size: bytes,
  s3_key: string
}

entities.name: SharedFile
entities.schema:
{
  share_id: UUID,
  file_id: UUID,
  owner_id: string,
  shared_with_id: string,
  permission: enum(READ|WRITE),
  created_at: timestamp
}

endpoints.response: { upload_id, chunk_urls: [presigned S3 URLs per chunk] }

Location: <CDN signed URL>
  (or presigned S3 URL for large files)

approaches.title: Single-Part Upload via API Server
approaches.description: Client uploads the entire file to the API server, which then writes it to S3. For a 5GB file at 10 Mbps, this takes 67 minutes and ties up an API server thread. Any network interruption requires restarting from scratch.
approaches.tradeoff: Not resumable; API server becomes bottleneck; poor for large files.

approaches.title: Direct S3 Multipart Upload
approaches.description: Client requests presigned URLs for each chunk from the API server. Client uploads chunks directly to S3 in parallel. Client notifies API server when all chunks are uploaded. S3 handles the storage; API server is only involved in orchestration. Resumable: failed chunks can be retried individually.
approaches.tradeoff: Client must manage chunk state; S3 multipart upload has 10,000 part limit.

approaches.title: Chunked Upload with Deduplication
approaches.description: Same as direct S3 multipart but with content-addressed deduplication: each chunk is identified by its SHA256 hash. Before uploading, the client sends chunk hashes to the server; the server returns which chunks already exist in S3 (delta sync). For a file where 90% of chunks are unchanged (e.g., appending to a document), only 10% of data is transferred. Chunks are stored once in S3 regardless of how many files reference them.
approaches.tradeoff: Dedup logic adds client complexity; SHA256 computation adds CPU overhead on client.

approaches.title: Polling
approaches.description: Desktop client polls the server every 30 seconds for file changes. 30-second delay is too slow for real-time sync. Polling at 1-second intervals for 500M users = 500M requests/sec — infeasible.
approaches.tradeoff: High latency or prohibitive server load.

approaches.title: Long Polling
approaches.description: Client holds an open HTTP connection; server responds when there are changes. Reduces unnecessary requests but still requires a connection per device. 500M users × 2 devices = 1B concurrent connections.
approaches.tradeoff: 1B concurrent connections requires enormous connection management infrastructure.

approaches.title: Server-Sent Events (SSE) via Notification Service
approaches.description: File changes publish events to Kafka. A Notification Service consumes Kafka and pushes change notifications to connected devices via SSE. SSE connections are lightweight (HTTP/2 multiplexed). The Notification Service is stateless — it maps user_id to SSE connection via a Redis pub/sub channel. On receiving a notification, the desktop client fetches only the changed chunks (delta sync).
approaches.tradeoff: SSE requires persistent connections; Redis pub/sub adds a hop; SSE not supported in all

components.name: File Service
components.responsibility: Orchestrates uploads; generates presigned S3 URLs; manages file metadata; handles versioning

components.name: S3
components.responsibility: Durable object storage for file chunks; lifecycle policies for old versions

components.name: CDN (CloudFront)
components.responsibility: Caches file downloads at edge; signed URLs for access control

components.name: Metadata DB (DynamoDB)
components.responsibility: Stores file metadata, chunk manifests, sharing permissions; partition key = owner_id

components.name: Redis Cache
components.responsibility: Caches hot file metadata; stores user→file change subscriptions for SSE

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Event bus for file change events; drives sync notifications

components.name: Notification Service
components.responsibility: Consumes Kafka; pushes SSE notifications to connected desktop/mobile clients

components.name: Cleanup Job
components.responsibility: Deletes old file versions beyond retention limit; removes orphaned chunks from S3

questions.title: 2. Conflict Resolution
questions.content:
When two devices modify the same file simultaneously, a conflict occurs. Dropbox's approach: the last write
wins for the file as a whole, but the losing version is saved as a 'conflicted copy' with the device name and
timestamp appended to the filename. This is simpler than operational transforms (used by Google Docs) and
acceptable for file-level sync where simultaneous edits are rare.

questions.title: 3. Storage Deduplication
questions.content:
Chunk-level deduplication is implemented using content-addressed storage: each chunk's S3 key is its SHA256
hash. A DynamoDB table maps chunk_hash → s3_key. Before uploading a chunk, the client checks if the hash
already exists. If it does, the upload is skipped and the file's chunk manifest simply references the existing S3
object. This achieves ~30% storage savings across a typical user base.

questions.title: 4. Access Control for Shared Files
questions.content:
Shared file access is enforced at the CDN layer using signed URLs with embedded permissions. When a user
requests a download, the File Service checks the SharedFiles table, verifies the requester has READ
permission, and generates a CloudFront signed URL valid for 1 hour. The URL includes the file_id and
permission level as signed parameters that CloudFront validates.

questions.title: 5. Versioning
questions.content:
Each file write creates a new version record in DynamoDB. The version table stores: file_id, version_number,
s3_key, size, checksum, created_at. The latest version is denormalized into the main file record for fast access.
Old versions beyond the retention limit (30 versions) are deleted by a background job that also removes the
corresponding S3 objects (if no other version references the same chunks).

keyFlows: ① Upload: Client → Gateway → File Svc → presigned URL → PUT to S3
② S3 event → File Svc updates metadata (status = uploaded)
③ Download: Gateway → CDN signed URL → edge cache (or S3 origin)
④ Sync: S3 event → Kafka → Notif Svc → WebSocket push to devices
⑤ Share: File Svc writes SharedFiles table (userId PK + fileId SK)

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study S3 multipart upload in depth: the 5MB minimum part size, the 10,000 part limit, how
ETags are used for integrity verification, and the lifecycle of an incomplete multipart upload.
Expert Note: Learn about content-addressed storage (CAS): how Git uses SHA1 hashes as object
identifiers, and how Dropbox's chunk deduplication is essentially a CAS system.
Expert Note: Understand Rabin fingerprinting for variable-size chunking: how it uses a rolling hash to find
content-dependent chunk boundaries, maximizing deduplication across file versions.
Expert Note: Study CloudFront signed URLs and signed cookies: how they embed expiry time and IP
restrictions, and how the private key signing process works.
Expert Note: Learn about the CAP theorem implications for file sync: why Dropbox chose availability over
consistency (last-write-wins with conflicted copies) rather than implementing distributed transactions.
